planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade; prairie spiderwort self-sows readily and naturalizes in prairies and open areas. The three violet-blue petals have a distinctive triangle formation typical of all Tradescantia species.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, average to dry soil in full sun to partial shade — prairie spiderwort is a Great Plains native Tradescantia closely related to the more widely grown Ohio spiderwort (T. ohiensis), producing the same distinctive three-petaled violet-blue to pink-purple flowers whose delicate petals last only a single morning before shriveling, but with new buds opening daily over many weeks in spring and early summer. Like all spiderworts, the clear blue-violet flowers are striking when massed, creating a wave of cool color in the spring garden. Prairie spiderwort is generally more compact and drought-tolerant than Ohio spiderwort, reflecting its origin on drier Great Plains prairies. Foliage goes dormant and yellows in midsummer heat — plant with later-emerging perennials to fill in. An excellent native for dry, sunny to partly shaded prairie gardens and informal settings in zones 3–9.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich to average, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; elm-leaved goldenrod is one of the most shade-tolerant goldenrods in the eastern US, blooming reliably in the conditions of an open woodland understory.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — elm-leaved goldenrod is an eastern North American native recognized by its leaves, which closely resemble elm leaves with strongly toothed, slightly rough-textured blades on short petioles. It grows naturally in moist, open woodland and woodland edges, making it one of a small number of goldenrods genuinely adapted to shaded conditions. The arching, one-sided (secund) racemes of small golden flowers appear in late summer through fall in shaded locations where most goldenrods cannot bloom. Like blue-stemmed goldenrod, it fills an important ecological niche by providing late-season nectar and pollen in shaded settings where pollinators searching for fall resources would otherwise find nothing. Spreads by rhizome to form a colony. An excellent native goldenrod for adding late-season ecological value to the woodland garden.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall or early spring in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to full shade; lyre-leaf sage is one of the most adaptable native sages, thriving in conditions ranging from full sun to fairly deep shade — and self-sowing reliably in lawns and woodland edges.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist to well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to full shade — lyre-leaf sage is one of the most adaptable and overlooked native sages, producing basal rosettes of deeply lobed, lyrate (lyre-shaped) leaves — often beautifully marked with dark purple blotches — followed by tall slender spikes of two-lipped, pale blue-violet flowers attractive to bumblebees, mason bees, and hummingbirds in spring and early summer. Unlike most sages, it thrives in shaded conditions including dry shade under trees, making it exceptionally useful for the difficult shaded, dry area where few flowering plants perform well. Self-sows freely in lawns (where it is native and naturalistic), woodland edges, and disturbed areas. The dark-mottled, rosette foliage is attractive in its own right through winter in the South. Traditional Appalachian medicinal plant. An outstanding, adaptable native sage for zones 5–9.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall (cold stratification improves germination) in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; tall bellflower is a biennial that blooms in its second year — establish a planting from seed in two successive years to ensure continuous bloom thereafter.

directions: Sow seed in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade — tall bellflower is a striking native biennial of eastern forest understories that can reach 3–6 feet tall when in bloom, producing a long terminal raceme and lateral branches covered with flat, five-lobed blue-violet flowers that open wide (unlike the nodding, bell-shaped flowers of most bellflowers) throughout summer. It is one of the most shade-tolerant native wildflowers capable of producing impressive flowering stems in quite deep shade. The long, curved styles and protruding stamens give the flowers a distinctive star-burst appearance. The plant is a true biennial — forming a rosette in year one, blooming and setting seed in year two, then dying. Self-sows reliably in appropriate conditions to maintain a naturalized colony. An outstanding native plant for creating a vertical accent in the shaded native garden in zones 3–8.

planting.note: Direct sow seed in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, dry to average, poor to average soil in full sun; gray goldenrod is the most drought-tolerant of the common eastern goldenrods, thriving in dry, sandy, and rocky soils where other goldenrods cannot establish.

directions: Sow seed in fall in well-drained, dry to average, poor to average soil in full sun — gray goldenrod is the most drought-tolerant of the common eastern North American goldenrods, taking its common name from the grayish, downy-hairy stems and leaves that help reduce water loss in its characteristic dry, open habitats: rocky outcrops, sandy barrens, dry roadsides, and dry open woodland edges throughout the eastern US and into the Great Plains. The one-sided arching racemes of golden yellow flowers curve gracefully toward the ground in late summer and fall, providing important late-season nectar for migrating monarch butterflies and a wide range of native bees. While not as showy as some taller goldenrods, gray goldenrod is invaluable for difficult dry, sandy, or rocky sites where creating a pollinator-supporting native planting is otherwise challenging. Historically used by Native American nations as a yellow dye plant.
